chunk_summary: This section discusses diagnostic cutoffs and laboratory evaluation for pediatric overweight and obesity, emphasizing age-specific criteria, risk stratification, and comorbidity screening.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific diagnostic cutoffs for pediatric overweight and obesity using WHO and IAP charts.
Identify clinical risk factors and comorbidities associated with pediatric obesity.
Learn laboratory evaluation protocols for cardiometabolic and liver-related comorbidities in overweight and obese children.
key_facts: For children under 5 years, overweight is defined as > +2 SD and obesity as > +3 SD on WHO weight-for-length/height z-scores.
For children aged 5–18 years, BMI-for-age is plotted on IAP charts, with South Asian-specific cutoffs of 23 (overweight risk) and 27 (obesity risk).
Initial risk stratification includes assessing blood pressure, puberty stage, sleep history, dietary patterns, and family history of metabolic diseases.
Laboratory evaluation includes fasting lipid profile, fasting glucose or HbA1c, and ALT for NAFLD in obese children.
Overweight children are tested for comorbidities only if additional risk factors, such as acanthosis nigricans or hypertension, are present.
Secondary causes of obesity should be considered if accompanied by short stature, delayed puberty, dysmorphic features, or rapid onset.
reference_quote: From 5–18 years, plot BMI-for-age on IAP charts and use adult-equivalent cut-offs; for South Asian children, the 23 (overweight risk) and 27 (obesity risk) lines support earlier risk detection.
